title: Track-Template Residual Features Instead Of Global Tyre Windows
summary: Official F1 strategy guides repeatedly show that pit windows are circuit-specific because pit-loss, degradation, warm-up, and overtaking differ by track.
rationale: Official F1 strategy guides repeatedly show that pit windows are circuit-specific because pit-loss, degradation, warm-up, and overtaking differ by track. A global expected-life map misses this. Leakage-safe race-template priors can capture that Mexico-like long hard stints and Monaco-like overcut tracks should produce different residual signals even with the same compound and tyre age.
implementation_hint: From training folds only, build race-level priors such as median first-stop lap, median stint length, and lap-window quantiles by `(Race, Compound, wet/dry)` and optionally `(Race, Compound, Position bucket)`. Feed the model residual features like `LapNumber - expected_first_stop_lap`, `TyreLife - expected_stint_age_at_this_progress`, and `distance_to_track_window_start/end`. Use fallback shrinkage to global priors for sparse races.
expected_effect: Should improve generalization across tracks by replacing hand-set compound constants with track-aware timing residuals.
risk: Small-sample races or format shifts across years can make templates noisy; use hierarchical shrinkage and year-aware backoff.
